title: Insular majuscule letterforms, the script of the Lindisfarne Gospels
subtitle: The compressed, rounded capital hand of early medieval Irish and Northumbrian manuscripts
excerpt: Insular majuscule is the script of the Book of Kells and the Lindisfarne Gospels. Its letterforms are rounded and compressed, written with a broad-edge nib held at roughly 20° and combined with elaborate wedge-shaped serifs. It is the ancestor of insular minuscule and the half-uncial forms.
sourceNotes: Edward Johnston, Writing and Illuminating and Lettering (1906), public domain, Internet Archive, chapters on insular scripts. Johnston analyses the Lindisfarne Gospels letter forms and provides reconstruction guidance. Available via Internet Archive.

glossaryTerms.definition: Scripts developed in the island monasteries of Ireland and Britain from the sixth century onward, distinct from the continental scripts. Characterised by compressed round forms, wedge-shaped entry serifs, and a distinctive treatment of certain letterforms (e.g. the half-uncial a and g).
A script transitional between the Roman uncial and the minuscule scripts. Letters begin to show ascenders and descenders, moving toward a true four-line system.

content.text: Insular majuscule takes its name from the island monasteries: the "insulae": of Ireland and Northumbria. Its wedge serifs, compressed forms, and elaborate initials mark it as distinct from any continental hand. The
family has two branches: this majuscule, used for display text, and the

items.description: Insular majuscule is written at approximately 4 nib-widths for the body height: a fairly tall letter for the nib size.

items.description: Ruled to 4 nib-widths for the body height. Insular majuscule has no ascenders or descenders in its pure form.

content.text: Hold the nib at approximately 20° from the horizontal: slightly higher than rustica, lower than Foundational. The pen grip is the same: between the index and middle finger, thumb on the side, hand resting on the outer edge of the little finger. The wedge serif that starts most insular letters is made by first placing the nib corner at the entry point, pulling a short diagonal hairline to the left, then placing the full nib width and pulling the main stroke. This two-part entry is the most important technique to practise before attempting full letterforms.

A: the characteristic insular A uses a flat top rather than a pointed apex. Stroke 1: a horizontal top stroke from left to right. Stroke 2: left diagonal from the left end of the top, pulling down to the baseline. Stroke 3: right diagonal from the right end of the top, pulling down to meet stroke 2 at the foot. Stroke 4: the crossbar at mid-height. The flat top and the downward-pulling diagonals are specific to the insular form.

D: a vertical stem with a rounded right shoulder. The bowl of the D is larger and rounder than in Roman capitals; it nearly touches a circle. E: wedge entry on the top arm, then the horizontal top arm, two cross-strokes (middle and bottom). The middle arm is shorter than the top and bottom. G: a C with a horizontal shelf added at the mid-height; in some insular forms the shelf extends inward rather than upward. H: two stems with a curved crossbar (arched rather than straight). I: a single vertical stroke with wedge serifs at top and bottom. M: four strokes: two vertical stems, two diagonal inner strokes meeting at the floor. N: two verticals and a curved diagonal (rounder than the Roman N). O: a full oval, compressed slightly more vertically than horizontally. R: a stem, a curved shoulder, a straight diagonal leg. The shoulder and leg almost but not quite join at the stem.
The most useful drills are the wedge entry stroke and the compressed oval: practise these individually before attempting letterforms. Write each letter at the same 4-nib-width height for consistency.
